_meta.purpose: Graph problem classifier — identify graph type, goal, and correct algorithm
_meta.usage: Stage 3I — triggered when input anatomy reveals graph structure
_meta.howToUse: First identify graph properties, then identify goal, then cross-reference for correct algorithm

graphProperties.name: Directed Graph
recognize: Edges have direction — A→B does not imply B→A
Problem uses words like 'from', 'to', 'one-way'
Prerequisites — course A must be taken before course B
Dependencies flow in one direction
implications: DFS cycle detection needs 3-color (not just visited boolean)
Topological sort is possible if DAG
SCC algorithms apply (Tarjan, Kosaraju)
In-degree and out-degree are distinct
graphProperties.watchOut: Do not use undirected Union Find for directed graph connectivity.

graphProperties.name: Undirected Graph
recognize: Edges are bidirectional — A-B means both directions
Problem uses words like 'connected', 'between', 'neighbors'
Friendship graphs, road networks, grid adjacency
implications: Cycle detection with simple visited boolean + parent tracking
Union Find works directly
No SCC — use connected components instead
Bipartite check with 2-coloring
graphProperties.watchOut: Track parent in DFS to avoid false cycle detection from back-edge to parent.

graphProperties.name: Weighted Graph
recognize: Edges have costs, distances, or times
Problem mentions travel time, cost, or distance
Not all paths are equal — want minimum cost path
implications: BFS no longer gives shortest path — use Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford
MST algorithms apply (Kruskal, Prim)
Weight sign matters — positive only vs negative weights
graphProperties.watchOut: Never use BFS for shortest path in weighted graph — gives wrong answer.

graphProperties.name: Unweighted Graph
recognize: All edges have equal cost (or cost = 1)
Minimum steps, hops, or moves
Grid movement with uniform cost
implications: BFS gives shortest path in O(V+E)
No need for priority queue
Level-order BFS = shortest distance
graphProperties.watchOut: If some edges cost 0 and others cost 1 — use 0-1 BFS with deque, not standard BFS.

graphProperties.name: DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)
recognize: Directed AND no cycles guaranteed
Dependencies always flow forward
Problem guarantees valid ordering exists
Tree is a special case of DAG
implications: Topological sort always possible
DP in topological order gives optimal paths
Longest path in DAG solvable in O(V+E)
No need for Bellman-Ford — no negative cycles possible
graphProperties.watchOut: Verify no cycles before assuming DAG. If cycles possible — must detect first.

graphProperties.name: Sparse Graph
recognize: E is close to V — E = O(V) or O(V log V)
Most nodes have low degree
Tree-like structure or grid with obstacles
implications: Adjacency list representation — O(V+E) space
Dijkstra with priority queue — O((V+E) log V)
Do not use adjacency matrix — wastes O(V^2) space
graphProperties.watchOut: Adjacency matrix for sparse graph wastes memory and slows cache performance.

graphProperties.name: Dense Graph
recognize: E is close to V^2 — most pairs of nodes connected
Complete or near-complete graph
V is small (V <= 1000) with many edges
implications: Adjacency matrix may be appropriate — O(1) edge lookup
Dijkstra with simple array — O(V^2) better than O((V+E) log V) when E = V^2
Floyd-Warshall feasible if V <= 500
graphProperties.watchOut: Dense graph with V=10^5 — adjacency matrix is 40GB. Use adjacency list regardless.

graphProperties.name: Negative Edge Weights
recognize: Edge weights can be negative
Problem mentions penalties or negative costs
Currency exchange with possible losses
implications: Dijkstra FAILS with negative edges — eliminate it
Use Bellman-Ford for single source shortest path
Use Floyd-Warshall for all pairs
Check for negative cycles — Bellman-Ford detects them
graphProperties.watchOut: Negative cycle makes shortest path undefined (can loop infinitely for lower cost).

graphProperties.name: Bipartite Graph
recognize: Nodes split into two sets — edges only between sets
2-colorable — no odd cycles
Matching problems — workers to jobs, students to schools
Conflict graph where conflicts only between different types
implications: Maximum matching via Hopcroft-Karp O(E sqrt(V))
Maximum independent set = V - maximum matching
Minimum vertex cover = maximum matching (König's theorem)
Check bipartiteness with BFS 2-coloring
graphProperties.watchOut: König's theorem only holds for bipartite graphs. Do not apply to general graphs.

graphGoals.name: Reachability — can I get from A to B?
recognize: Is node B reachable from node A?
Are all nodes reachable from source?
Is graph connected?

algorithms.name: BFS
algorithms.notes: Also gives shortest path as bonus

algorithms.name: DFS
algorithms.notes: Simpler to implement recursively

algorithms.name: Union Find
algorithms.notes: Best for dynamic connectivity — edges added over time

graphGoals.watchOut: For directed graphs — reachability is not symmetric. A reaches B does not mean B reaches A.

graphGoals.name: Shortest Path — single source
recognize: Minimum distance from one source to all nodes
Minimum distance from source to specific target
Minimum steps, cost, or time from start

algorithms.name: BFS
algorithms.notes: Fastest for unweighted — never use Dijkstra for unweighted

algorithms.name: 0-1 BFS
algorithms.notes: Use deque — push to front for 0-weight, back for 1-weight

algorithms.name: Dijkstra with Priority Queue
algorithms.when: Weighted graph, all non-negative weights, sparse
algorithms.notes: Standard choice for weighted shortest path

algorithms.name: Dijkstra with Array
algorithms.when: Dense graph — E close to V^2
algorithms.notes: Better than PQ when E = O(V^2)

algorithms.name: Bellman-Ford
algorithms.notes: Also detects negative cycles — if relaxation still possible after V-1 rounds

algorithms.name: SPFA
algorithms.notes: Faster in practice but same worst case. Hackable in competitive programming.

graphGoals.watchOut: Dijkstra with negative edges gives wrong answer silently — no error, just incorrect result.

graphGoals.name: Shortest Path — all pairs
recognize: Distance between every pair of nodes needed
Multiple source-destination queries
Small graph V <= 500

algorithms.name: Floyd-Warshall
algorithms.when: V <= 500, handles negative edges (not negative cycles)
algorithms.notes: Simple 3-loop DP. dist[i][j] = min path from i to j.

algorithms.name: Dijkstra from each node
algorithms.notes: Better than Floyd-Warshall for sparse graphs

algorithms.name: Johnson's Algorithm
algorithms.notes: Reweights edges to remove negatives, then runs Dijkstra from each node

graphGoals.watchOut: Floyd-Warshall at V=1000 is 10^9 ops — too slow. Use at V <= 500 only.

graphGoals.name: Minimum Spanning Tree
recognize: Connect all nodes with minimum total edge cost
No cycles in result
N-1 edges for N nodes

algorithms.name: Kruskal
algorithms.when: Sparse graph — sort edges, add if no cycle (Union Find)
algorithms.notes: Simpler to implement. Better for sparse graphs.

algorithms.name: Prim with Priority Queue
algorithms.when: Dense graph — grow tree from source
algorithms.notes: Better for dense graphs

graphGoals.watchOut: MST algorithms only work on undirected graphs. For directed graphs use minimum cost arborescence.

graphGoals.name: Topological Sort
recognize: Need linear ordering respecting all dependencies
Course schedule — what order to take courses
Build order — what to compile first
Graph is guaranteed to be DAG or need to check

algorithms.name: Kahn's Algorithm (BFS)
algorithms.notes: Process nodes with in-degree 0. If not all nodes processed — cycle exists.

algorithms.name: DFS post-order
algorithms.notes: Add node to result AFTER visiting all descendants. Reverse result at end.

graphGoals.watchOut: If graph has cycle — topological sort is impossible. Kahn's detects this: processed count < V.

graphGoals.name: Cycle Detection
recognize: Does graph contain a cycle?
Can all tasks be completed (no circular dependency)?
Detect back edges in DFS

algorithms.name: DFS 3-color
algorithms.notes: White=unvisited, Gray=in-stack, Black=done. Gray→Gray edge = cycle.

algorithms.name: DFS with parent tracking
algorithms.notes: Back edge to non-parent node = cycle.

algorithms.name: Union Find
algorithms.notes: If union of two nodes already in same component — cycle found.

algorithms.name: Floyd's Cycle Detection
algorithms.when: Linked list or functional graph (each node has one outgoing edge)
algorithms.notes: Slow and fast pointer. Meets inside cycle.

graphGoals.watchOut: DFS 3-color is for directed graphs only. Using it on undirected gives false positives.

graphGoals.name: Connected Components
recognize: How many separate groups exist?
Which nodes belong to same component?
Dynamic — components merge as edges added

algorithms.name: BFS / DFS
algorithms.when: Static graph — all edges known upfront
algorithms.notes: Count DFS/BFS calls from unvisited nodes

algorithms.name: Union Find
algorithms.when: Dynamic — edges added one by one
algorithms.notes: Best for online connectivity queries

algorithms.name: Tarjan / Kosaraju
algorithms.notes: SCC ≠ connected components. Different algorithms needed.

graphGoals.watchOut: Connected components (undirected) and SCC (directed) are completely different problems.

graphGoals.name: Strongly Connected Components (SCC)
recognize: Directed graph — find groups where every node reaches every other
Condensation graph needed
2-SAT problem (reduces to SCC)

algorithms.name: Tarjan's Algorithm
algorithms.notes: Outputs SCCs in reverse topological order of condensation DAG

algorithms.name: Kosaraju's Algorithm
algorithms.notes: Pass 1: DFS on original, record finish order. Pass 2: DFS on reversed graph in reverse finish order.

graphGoals.watchOut: After SCC condensation — result is always a DAG. Can then apply DAG algorithms.

graphGoals.name: Bridges and Articulation Points
recognize: Which edges if removed disconnect the graph?
Which nodes if removed disconnect the graph?
Critical connections in network

algorithms.name: Tarjan's Bridge Finding
algorithms.notes: Edge (u,v) is bridge if low[v] > disc[u]

algorithms.name: Tarjan's Articulation Points
algorithms.notes: Node u is articulation point if it has child v with low[v] >= disc[u]

graphGoals.watchOut: Both use DFS with disc[] and low[] arrays. low[v] = minimum discovery time reachable from subtree of v.

graphGoals.name: Bipartite Matching
recognize: Assign items from set A to items from set B
Each item can be matched to at most one other
Maximize number of matched pairs

algorithms.name: Hungarian Algorithm (Augmenting Path)
algorithms.notes: Find augmenting paths with DFS. Each path increases matching by 1.

algorithms.name: Hopcroft-Karp
algorithms.when: Large bipartite graphs — faster than Hungarian
algorithms.notes: Find all shortest augmenting paths in each BFS phase

graphGoals.watchOut: Verify graph is bipartite before applying matching. Non-bipartite needs general matching (much harder).

graphGoals.name: Shortest Path in Grid
recognize: Movement in 2D grid — up/down/left/right
Minimum steps to reach target cell
Some cells blocked or have different costs

algorithms.name: BFS
algorithms.when: Uniform cost movement — all steps cost 1
algorithms.notes: Most common grid shortest path. Add (r,c) to queue, mark visited.

algorithms.name: Dijkstra on grid
algorithms.notes: Treat each cell as a node with cost to enter it

algorithms.name: 0-1 BFS on grid
algorithms.notes: Deque — free moves to front, cost-1 moves to back

algorithms.name: A* Search
algorithms.notes: Faster than Dijkstra in practice for single target but same worst case

graphGoals.watchOut: BFS on grid with obstacles — always check bounds AND visited before pushing to queue.

graphGoals.name: Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA)
recognize: Given tree — find common ancestor of two nodes
Distance between two nodes in tree
Path between two nodes in tree

algorithms.name: Naive DFS
algorithms.notes: Find path from root to both nodes, find divergence point

algorithms.name: Binary Lifting
algorithms.complexity: O(n log n) preprocessing, O(log n) per query
algorithms.when: Many LCA queries — q up to 10^5
algorithms.notes: Precompute 2^k ancestors for each node. Jump up in powers of 2.

algorithms.name: Euler Tour + Sparse Table (RMQ)
algorithms.complexity: O(n log n) preprocessing, O(1) per query
algorithms.when: Extremely many queries needing O(1) response
algorithms.notes: Convert LCA to RMQ on Euler tour array

graphGoals.watchOut: Binary lifting requires rooted tree. Re-root if needed before preprocessing.

algorithmSelector.reason: Unweighted graph — BFS level order gives shortest path
Weighted, no negatives, sparse — standard Dijkstra
Dense graph — array Dijkstra beats PQ when E = O(V^2)
All pairs needed, V small enough for O(V^3)
DAG topological sort — process by in-degree
Sparse undirected — sort edges and Union Find
Dynamic connectivity — edges added over time

commonMistakes.mistake: Using BFS on weighted graph for shortest path
commonMistakes.consequence: Wrong answer — BFS ignores edge weights
commonMistakes.fix: Use Dijkstra for non-negative weights, Bellman-Ford for negative weights

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Dijkstra with negative edge weights
commonMistakes.consequence: Wrong answer — Dijkstra assumes once a node is finalized its distance is optimal
commonMistakes.fix: Use Bellman-Ford or handle negative weights with Johnson's reweighting

commonMistakes.mistake: DFS cycle detection in undirected graph without tracking parent
commonMistakes.consequence: False positive — edge to parent reported as cycle
commonMistakes.fix: Pass parent node to DFS. Only flag back edge if neighbor is not parent.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using Union Find for directed graph connectivity
commonMistakes.consequence: Wrong answer — Union Find does not respect edge direction
commonMistakes.fix: Use DFS/BFS with directed edges for directed connectivity
Floyd-Warshall only for V <= 500. Use repeated Dijkstra for larger V.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not checking grid bounds before pushing to BFS queue
commonMistakes.consequence: Array out of bounds or infinite loop
commonMistakes.fix: Always check 0 <= r < rows AND 0 <= c < cols before processing neighbor

commonMistakes.mistake: Assuming topological sort succeeds without checking for cycles
commonMistakes.consequence: Incomplete sort returned as valid — wrong answer
commonMistakes.fix: After Kahn's algorithm — check if processed count == V. If not — cycle exists.
